title: BLEU and COMET Metrics
concept: BLEU checks word overlap; modern COMET uses neural embeddings to evaluate translation quality based on semantic meaning, better capturing synonyms.

exercises.prompt: What are BLEU and COMET metrics used for in AI?

options.text: To generate images from text descriptions
To evaluate the quality of machine translations
To compress audio files into smaller sizes

exercises.explain: BLEU and COMET are metrics used in Natural Language Processing (NLP) to measure how well machine translation systems perform compared to human translations.

cards.title: Lost in Translation
cards.body: If you translate "It is raining cats and dogs" into another language, a word-by-word translation makes no sense! How do we teach computers to evaluate if a translation actually sounds natural and keeps the original meaning, instead of just checking exact words? Let's find out!

cards.title: What are BLEU and COMET?
cards.body:
BLEU is an older metric that checks for exact word overlap between a machine translation and a human translation. 

COMET is a modern metric that uses AI (neural embeddings) to evaluate the actual meaning behind the words, letting it recognize when different words share the same meaning.

cards.title: The Two Language Teachers
cards.body:
Imagine evaluating a student's book report. 

A strict teacher (BLEU) just counts how many exact words they copied from the summary sheet. If they used a synonym like 'huge' instead of 'large', the teacher marks it wrong. 

A smart teacher (COMET) reads for meaning. They understand that 'huge' and 'large' mean the same thing, grading the student on their actual understanding.

cards.title: Spotting the Difference
cards.body:
Let's look at a translation of a French sentence:
Original: "Le chat est petit."

Human Reference: "The cat is small."
Machine Translation A: "The cat is tiny."
Machine Translation B: "The cat is small."

BLEU gives Translation B a perfect score, but penalizes Translation A because 'tiny' does not match 'small' exactly. 

COMET gives both high scores because it understands that 'tiny' and 'small' mean the same thing in this context.

cards.title: The Exact Match Trap
cards.body: It is easy to think that a translation metric must find exact word matches to be accurate. In reality, language is full of synonyms. A translation can be absolutely perfect even if it doesn't use the exact same words as the human reference! That is why semantic tools like COMET are becoming the new standard.

cards.title: BLEU vs. COMET in Action
cards.body: See how these two metrics judge the translation: "The movie was fantastic" compared to the human reference: "The film was great."

left.text: Low score. 'Movie' doesn't match 'film', and 'fantastic' doesn't match 'great'. Zero points for those words!

right.text: High score. The neural network understands that 'movie/film' and 'fantastic/great' carry the same meaning.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of using the BLEU metric to evaluate translations?

options.text: It requires too much computer graphics power.
It only looks for exact word matches and fails to understand synonyms.
It only works for translations between English and Spanish.
It changes the meaning of the translated text.

exercises.explain: BLEU only checks if the exact same words appear in both translations, completely missing synonyms or paraphrases.

exercises.prompt: COMET is better at understanding synonyms than BLEU because COMET evaluates the meaning of sentences using neural networks.
exercises.explain: This is true. BLEU is a rule-based algorithm that counts matching words, whereas COMET uses neural network embeddings to compare the actual meanings.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing metric name:
exercises.sentence: While BLEU looks for exact word matches, [blank] uses neural embeddings to check if the translation has the same meaning.
exercises.explain: COMET evaluates translations based on their semantic meaning using neural networks, whereas BLEU is limited to checking exact word-for-word overlaps.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations of a translation score comes from a modern semantic metric like COMET?

options.text: I calculated the overlap of 3-grams and 4-grams to give this a score of 0.65.
I analyzed the neural embeddings of both sentences to confirm they convey the same meaning.
I counted the exact words that match between the candidate and reference texts.

exercises.explain: The second option is generated by an AI metric like COMET because it goes beyond word matching to analyze the deep semantic meaning and quality of the sentence using neural networks.

exercises.prompt: Your team is building a translation app and notices that BLEU gives a bad score to a perfect translation just because it used the word 'tiny' instead of 'small'. Why does upgrading to COMET solve this problem?

options.text: COMET counts exact word matches faster than BLEU.
COMET uses neural embeddings to compare meanings, allowing it to recognize synonyms.
COMET only checks the first and last words of a sentence.
COMET ignores the original source text completely.

exercises.explain: COMET uses neural network embeddings to compare the meaning (semantics) of the machine translation against both the human reference translation and the original source text, allowing it to recognize accurate synonyms. BLEU is limited to matching exact words.
